title: States’ Education Reclamation Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.
plain_summary: This bill would shut down the Department of Education completely. Some education programs would move to other government departments like the Department of Labor or Department of Health and Human Services. States would get block grants with the same amount of money they currently receive from federal education programs, but they could spend it on any education purpose their state laws allow. States would have much more control over how to use education money with very little oversight from the federal government. The bill also asks for reports on whether the federal government should reduce taxes and stop funding education programs entirely.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Education and Workforce.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the States Education Reclamation Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress believes the following facts are true. The Constitution gives states and local people the power to make decisions about education policy. A federal Department of Education goes against these constitutional principles. Parents, teachers, and local communities have traditionally run schools in the United States, and they do this job best. Students are not learning well under government management. The Department of Education has made it harder for parents to make important choices about their children's education. It has also weakened the ability of communities to control their own schools. The Department of Education started forty one years ago with a budget of fourteen billion dollars. Now it spends almost seventy three and a half billion dollars each year running about one hundred programs. During this same time, how well seventeen year old students perform has not improved since nineteen seventy one. The Department of Education has created too many rules, made schools too similar to each other, added too much paperwork, and caused too many lawsuits in American education. The Department of Education spends large amounts of money to keep itself running. The average public school teacher in America earns sixty one thousand seven hundred thirty dollars per year. The average Department of Education employee earns one hundred twelve thousand seven hundred twenty four dollars per year. In some states, public school teachers earn less than the national average. In North Carolina, the average public school teacher earns fifty three thousand nine hundred seventy five dollars per year. Recent test results show that American students do poorly in math, science, and reading compared to students from other countries. Only when parents and local communities have the power to act can the United States improve education to an acceptable level. The current system where the federal government makes uniform education rules hurts local businesses and communities, does not meet the economic needs of states, and makes it harder for America to compete with other countries for jobs. The Department of Education has been against many promising changes to education, including changes that would give more power to parents, teachers, and local communities. The United States used to be a place where states could try new ideas in education. Now the country is moving toward making all education the same, which does not consider the different educational needs of our diverse students.

sections.title: Section 3 Abolition of Department of Education
sections.text: The Department of Education is shut down completely. Any program that the Secretary of Education or the Department of Education runs according to law is ended, except for programs that are moved to other departments under section seven. This includes ending all programs under the Department of Education Organization Act and the General Education Provisions Act.

sections.title: Section 4 Grants to States for Elementary and Secondary and for Postsecondary Education Programs
sections.text: Each state has the right to receive money from the Secretary of the Treasury by no later than July first of the year before the budget year. For budget year two thousand twenty five and each year after that through two thousand thirty three, each state will get a grant equal to the amount of federal money the state got for elementary and secondary school programs in two thousand twenty five, except for money being moved to other departments under section seven. Each state will also get a grant equal to the amount of federal money the state got for college and university programs in two thousand twenty five, except for money being moved to other departments under section seven. Money from the United States Treasury that has not been set aside for other purposes will pay for these grants from two thousand twenty five through two thousand thirty three. The Secretary of the Treasury will send grant money directly to each state following the rules in section six thousand five hundred three of title thirty one of the United States Code. States must spend the money they receive in the same budget year they get it or in the following budget year. States can use elementary and secondary school grant money for any elementary or secondary education purpose allowed by state law, including raising teacher salaries. States can use college and university grant money for any college or university education purpose allowed by state law. Grant money can only be used to add to the amount of money the state would spend on education from non federal sources if they did not get the grant. Grant money cannot replace money the state would have spent anyway.

sections.title: Section 5 Administrative and Fiscal Accountability
sections.text: By October first two thousand twenty five and every year after that, each state must hire an approved auditing company to check how the state spent grant money from the budget year ending September thirtieth of that year. The auditing company will review all state spending and transfers of grant money to see if the state followed the rules in section four. The audit must be done by a company that is approved by the Secretary of the Treasury, approved by the state's chief executive officer, and independent of any federal, state, or local government agency. The audit must follow generally accepted auditing principles. By April thirtieth two thousand twenty six and every year after that, each state must send the audit results to the state legislature and to the Secretary of the Treasury for the budget year ending September thirtieth of that year. If an audit finds that a state broke the rules about how to spend grant money, the state must pay back one hundred percent of the money it used incorrectly as a penalty to the United States Treasury. If a state does not pay this penalty, the Secretary of the Treasury will subtract the unpaid amount from future grants to that state. By January thirty first two thousand twenty six and every year after that, each state must send a report to the Secretary of the Treasury and the state legislature about what the state did with grant money during the most recently completed budget year. The report must include the audit results, specify how much grant money the state received, and be in a format that accurately describes what the state did with the money and provides a complete record of how the money was spent. States must make these reports available on a public website and provide copies in other formats when people ask for them. The Secretary of the Treasury will not send grant money to a state if the state does not submit its required audit on time, does not submit its required report on time, or breaks the rules in section four until the state fixes the problem. The Secretary of the Treasury can only make rules and guidance documents needed to implement auditing company approval, penalties, and payment withholding. The Secretary can also make grant payments to states, approve auditing companies, withhold or reduce grant payments when required, and enforce nondiscrimination rules specified in section six. The Attorney General can only supervise grant money as allowed in section six. Nothing in this section limits a state's power to pursue civil and criminal penalties under state law against any person or organization that misuses grant money or commits fraud or abuse related to grant money.

sections.title: Section 6 Nondiscrimination Provisions
sections.text: No person can be excluded from participating in, denied benefits from, or discriminated against in any program or activity funded completely or partially with grant money paid to a state under section four because of their disability under section five hundred four of the Rehabilitation Act of nineteen seventy three, their sex under title nine of the Education Amendments of nineteen seventy two, or their race, color, or national origin under title six of the Civil Rights Act of nineteen sixty four. If the Attorney General decides that a state or an organization that received grant money has failed to follow these nondiscrimination laws, the Secretary of the Treasury will notify the state's chief executive officer about the failure and ask the chief executive officer to fix the problem. If the state's chief executive officer does not fix the compliance problem within sixty days after receiving notification, the Attorney General can file a lawsuit or use the enforcement powers provided under the Rehabilitation Act of nineteen seventy three, the Education Amendments of nineteen seventy two, or the Civil Rights Act of nineteen sixty four as appropriate.

sections.title: Section 7 Transfer of Certain Department of Education Programs
sections.text: Within twenty four months after this law is passed, several Department of Education programs will be moved to other government departments. All job training programs, including those under the Carl D Perkins Career and Technical Education Act of two thousand six, will be moved to the Department of Labor. All special education grant programs under the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act will be moved to the Department of Health and Human Services. All Indian education programs will be moved to the Department of the Interior. All Impact Aid programs under title eight of the Elementary and Secondary Education Act of nineteen sixty five will be moved to the Department of Defense. The Federal Pell Grant program under title four of the Higher Education Act of nineteen sixty five will be moved to the Department of the Treasury. All federal student loan programs will be moved to the Department of the Treasury. All programs under the Institute of Education Sciences will be moved to the Department of Health and Human Services. All programs under the D.C. Opportunity Scholarship Program will be moved to the Department of Health and Human Services. These transfers only move the administrative responsibility for running the programs as provided by law or delegation of authority. The transfers do not move Department of Education employees who work on these programs to the new departments.

sections.title: Section 8 GAO Report
sections.text: Within ninety days after this law is passed, the Comptroller General of the United States must send a report to the Committee on Education and the Workforce of the House of Representatives and the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions of the Senate. The report must include a review and evaluation of whether it is possible to help states and local communities fund education better by reducing federal taxes and eliminating federal government involvement in providing grants for education programs. The report must also evaluate whether the federal agencies that will take over the transferred programs can successfully maintain those programs.

sections.title: Section 9 Plan for Closure of the Department of Education
sections.text: Within three hundred sixty five days after this law is passed, the President must send Congress a plan to close the Department of Education according to this law.

sections.title: Section 10 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, elementary school and secondary school have the same meanings given in section eight thousand one hundred one of the Elementary and Secondary Education Act of nineteen sixty five. Institution of higher education has the same meaning given in section one hundred two of the Elementary and Secondary Education Act of nineteen sixty five. State has the same meaning given in section one hundred three of the Higher Education Act of nineteen sixty five.
